elements.sent-2411-001: We theoretically investigate the finite-time performance of a quantum thermochemical engine utilizing a harmonically trapped one-dimensional (1D) Bose gas in the quasicondensate regime as the working fluid.
elements.sent-2411-002: The proposed engine operates in an Otto cycle, where the unitary work strokes are simulated through a quench of the interatomic interactions of 1D Bose gas.
elements.sent-2411-003: In the work strokes, the working fluid is treated as a closed quantum many-body system that undergoes dynamic evolution, beginning from an initial thermal equilibrium state at a non-zero temperature.
elements.sent-2411-004: On the other hand, during the thermalization strokes, the working fluid is treated as an open many-body quantum system in diffusive contact with a thermal reservoir, allowing particle exchange alongside the transfer of heat.
elements.sent-2411-005: Using a $c$-field approach, we demonstrate that the engine's operation is enabled by the chemical work done on the working fluid through the flow of particles from the hot reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-006: We examine the proposed quantum thermochemical engine's finite-time performance in two extreme regimes: (i) the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime, which yields maximum power while compromising efficiency, and (ii) the quasistatic (near-adiabatic) limit, which approaches maximum efficiency but generates zero power output due to slow driving of the system.
elements.sent-2411-007: Notably, we show that utilizing chemical work makes achieving maximum efficiency possible even in the out-of-equilibrium regime while simultaneously operating at maximum power.
elements.sent-2411-008: Thus, in the out-of-equilibrium operational regime, our proposed engine provides a favourable trade-off between efficiency and power output.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also draw connections to previous research, particularly the case of an adiabatic engine cycle at zero temperature.
elements.sent-2411-010: We show that this zero-temperature scenario establishes an upper bound on the efficiency and work of our proposed thermochemical engine operating at non-zero temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-011: Quantum engines (QEs) \cite{bouton2021quantum, koch2022making, myers2020bosons,nautiyal2024finite, fogarty2020many, chen2019interaction, simmons2023thermodynamic,boubakour2023interaction, keller2020feshbach, li2018efficient, li2021shortcut, singh2020optimal, shastri2022optimization, Gashu, sajitha2024quantumthermalmachineregimes, pozas2018quantum, singh2023thermodynamic, singh2022unified,singh2020performance,singh2019three, ptaszynski2022non, pedram2023quantum, williamson2023many offer an ideal platform for testing the fundamental laws of thermodynamics in the quantum regime.}
elements.sent-2411-012: One-dimensional (1D) Bose gases are a promising candidate for use as an interacting many-body working fluid in QEs \cite{chen2019interaction, li2018efficient, fogarty2020many, keller2020feshbach, nautiyal2024finite, nautiyal2024classical, chen2018bose, estrada2024quantum, charalambous2019heat, boubakour2023interaction .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The experimental accessibility and precise control over extreme physical parameter regimes in a 1D Bose gas of ultracold atoms makes it suitable for performing quantum simulations in experimentally realisable scenarios \cite{bloch2012quantum, cornish2024quantum, gross2017quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In an interacting 1D Bose gas, exotic many-body quantum effects such as quantum correlations \cite{carollo2020nonequilibrium, xiao2023quantum , many-body interactions \cite{chen2019interaction, jaramillo2016quantum, nautiyal2024finite, keller2020feshbach, boubakour2023interaction , quantum criticality \cite{fogarty2020many , many-body localisation \cite{halpern2019quantum and quantum statistics \cite{koch2022making can be exploited to facilitate operation as a QE and enhance the performance of such engines.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: QEs with interacting many-body systems as the working fluid can operate in multiple ways that are not typically possible with QEs with non-interacting working fluids.
elements.sent-2411-016: For example, quenching the interaction strength can enable work to be performed on or extracted from the working fluid, similar to the compression and expansion work strokes in a traditional Otto engine cycle \cite{chen2019interaction, nautiyal2024finite, keller2020feshbach, li2018efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Further, unlike a conventional Otto cycle, which relies on coupling with a thermal reservoir to increase the system's internal energy, an interacting quantum system can achieve this by quenching the interaction strength.
elements.sent-2411-018: This process alters the system's internal energy independently, without necessitating heat exchange with a reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-019: This increase in internal energy can then be harnessed to extract work from the system, as demonstrated in recent experimental studies \cite{simmons2023thermodynamic, koch2022making .}
elements.sent-2411-020: A trade-off exists between the power output and efficiency of a QE when operated in finite-time \cite{campbell2017trade, campisi2016power, shiraishi2016universal, singh2022unified, kaur2024performance, deng2013boosting .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Generally, as the engine's driving time increases, irreversible work is produced due to non-adiabatic excitations, leading to a reduction in efficiency \cite{keller2020feshbach, shiraishi2016universal, abah2019shortcut,chand2021finite .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Conversely, irreversible work is minimized when the QE operates in the adiabatic (quasi-static) limit, allowing the engine to reach maximum efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, the infinitely long driving time in this scenario results in zero power output \cite{li2018efficient, abah2019shortcut, mukherjee2021many .}
elements.sent-2411-024: A central challenge in quantum thermodynamics is designing QEs that optimize the trade-off between efficiency and power output.
elements.sent-2411-025: The aim is to operate with near-maximum (near-adiabatic) efficiency while maintaining non-zero power output in finite-time engine operation \cite{andresen2011current,kaur2024performance, nautiyal2024finite, keller2020feshbach, li2018efficient, raz2016geometric, chen2024optimal, shastri2022optimization .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In recent studies on QEs using interacting Bose gases, efforts to enhance finite-time performance have primarily focused on implementing shortcut to adiabaticity (STA) protocols \cite{guery2019shortcuts, abah2019shortcut, keller2020feshbach, li2018efficient, fogarty2020many, campbell2017trade .}
elements.sent-2411-027: While promising, STA protocols face notable limitations, including challenges with modulation instability \cite{keller2020feshbach, li2018efficient , increased energy requirements for practical deployment \cite{calzetta2018not , and significant experimental constraints \cite{chen2010transient .}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, exploring alternative, simpler methods to achieve near-maximum efficiency while maintaining non-zero power output has become increasingly valuable (e.g., see \cite{revathy2024improving ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: QEs operating in out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regimes \cite{wiedmann2020non, kaur2024performance, nautiyal2024finite, koyanagi2024classical, pezzutto2019out, singh2020performance, carollo2020nonequilibrium, denzler2024nonequilibrium, watson2024interaction,arezzo2024many, paulino2023nonequilibrium, paneru2018optimal,carollo2020nonequilibrium2 have garnered significant attention in recent years for three main reasons.}
elements.sent-2411-030: First, they are experimentally realizable with precise control over external parameters \cite{carollo2020nonequilibrium and hence offer a realistic evaluation of the engine performance by accounting for non-adiabatic effects during the finite-time driving of the work strokes \cite{kaur2024performance, singh2020performance, nautiyal2024finite, carollo2020nonequilibrium .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, work strokes are implemented in such engines in the shortest time, providing an opportunity to maximize the power output \cite{nautiyal2024finite, arezzo2024many, bhandari2022continuous,paneru2018optimal .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Third, studies of non-equilibrium QEs can be extended to include non-equilibrium dynamics resulting from strong coupling between the working fluid and the reservoir during the thermalization strokes \cite{nautiyal2024finite, pezzutto2019out,newman2020quantum, kaneyasu2023quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The impact of these non-equilibrium dynamics on engine performance is model-dependent and can either be advantageous or detrimental.
elements.sent-2411-034: For example, in reference \cite{newman2020quantum , it was shown that strong coupling between the working fluid and the reservoir in non-equilibrium QEs induces system-reservoir correlations that reduce the efficiency and power output.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Conversely, references \cite{wiedmann2020non, kaneyasu2023quantum demonstrate that QEs operating in out-of-equilibrium regimes can harness quantum correlations between the working fluid and reservoirs to improve performance under certain conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Even though non-equilibrium QEs, in general, have attracted attention, only a handful of studies have examined the performance of an out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) QE with interacting 1D Bose gas as the working fluid.
elements.sent-2411-037: Only two recent studies have provided preliminary insights to the best of our knowledge \cite{watson2024interaction, nautiyal2024finite .}
elements.sent-2411-038: This gap underscores the need to explore such systems further, especially to understand how efficiency and power trade-offs manifest under non-equilibrium conditions and determine the upper bound on the performance of such engines in realistic scenarios.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.45]{Fig1.png} \caption{Schematic diagram of the proposed quantum thermochemical Otto engine cycle driven by inter-atomic interactions in a weakly interacting harmonically trapped 1D Bose gas. The Otto cycle operates between two interaction strengths denoted as $g_c$ and $g_h$. The unitary work strokes, labelled BC and DA, are represented in grey. The diffusive thermalization strokes, AB and CD, are colour-coded to reflect their connection to the cold (blue) and hot (red) reservoirs, corresponding to temperatures $T_c$ and $T_h$, respectively. Since $g \simeq 2\hbar \omega_{\perp}a$, modifying the transverse confinement frequency $\omega_{\perp}$ enables the tuning of $g$. By increasing or decreasing $\omega_{\perp}$, the work strokes DA and BC become analogous to the (transverse) compression and expansion strokes of a traditional volumetric Otto cycle. } \label{fig:Fig1}
elements.sent-2411-039: This work investigates the finite-time performance of an experimentally realizable quantum thermochemical engine (QTE) operating in a non-equilibrium regime.
elements.sent-2411-040: The proposed QTE operates an Otto cycle, where the expansion and compression strokes are implemented through a sudden quench of inter-particle interactions in a weakly interacting 1D Bose gas in the quasicondensate \cite{bouchoule2016finite,kheruntsyan2005finite regime.}
elements.sent-2411-041: During the thermalization strokes, the working fluid is in diffusive contact with a thermal reservoir, allowing chemical work in the form of particle flow in addition to heat energy.
elements.sent-2411-042: We demonstrate that the operation as an engine is enabled by this chemical work, where additional particles from the hot reservoir are introduced into the system.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, we benchmark the performance of the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) engine with that of a quasistatic (near-adiabatic) engine, which achieves maximum efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-044: Additionally, we extend the results of reference \cite{keller2020feshbach to the finite-temperature regime of 1D Bose gas.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We relate our findings to prior research in reference \cite{keller2020feshbach , which analyzed the performance of a similar interaction-driven chemical engine in the adiabatic (maximum efficiency) regime with a zero-temperature Bose gas.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We show that these earlier results at zero temperature explored in reference \cite{keller2020feshbach provide an upper bound on the efficiency of our proposed QTE when operating at finite (non-zero) temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Finally, we show that by performing thermochemical work—specifically, increasing the number of particles exchanged with the thermal reservoir— our proposed QTE can achieve near-maximum efficiency even in the sudden quench regime without the need for optimization protocols such as shortcuts to adiabaticity, as employed in reference \cite{keller2020feshbach .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The structure of this article is as follows: Section \ref{sec:2 introduces the model of the Otto cycle and outlines the $c$-field approach used to simulate the finite-time engine cycle.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Section \ref{sec:3 presents the main results of the paper, where we evaluate the performance of the QTE and quantify its figures of merit.}
elements.sent-2411-050: We evaluate key metrics such as power output, efficiency, and the power-efficiency trade-off as the duration of the work strokes shifts from the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime to the quasistatic (near-adiabatic) regime.
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, Section \ref{sec:conclusion provides a summary of our results and discusses potential future directions.}
elements.sent-2411-052: The Otto engine cycle, illustrated in Fig.\ref{fig:Fig1 , is frequently studied in quantum thermodynamics due to its simplistic design \cite{Myers2022quantum and close resemblance to real-world engine cycles \cite{schroeder2020introduction .}}}

elements.sent-2411-055: The thermalization strokes, AB and CD, involve coupling the working fluid to hot and cold thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_h$ and $T_c$, respectively, while keeping the volume constant.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Timescales corresponding to the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) and quasistatic regime of engine operation
elements.sent-2411-116: We explore the performance of the proposed quantum Otto engine in two extreme regimes of operation with respect to the duration of the work strokes.
elements.sent-2411-117: These regimes are: (i) the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime, where work strokes are completed in the shortest possible time, inducing maximum irreversible work due to non-adiabatic excitations, resulting in a minimum efficiency \cite{born1928beweis,abah2019shortcut,li2021shortcut,del2013shortcuts, rezek2006irreversible ; (ii) the quasistatic (near-adiabatic) regime, which allows for near-maximum efficiency at the cost of minimal power, as it requires extremely slow driving of the system during work strokes to minimize the non-adiabatic excitations \cite{born1928beweis, guery2019shortcuts, li2021shortcut .}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Theoretically, a sudden quench is often treated as an instantaneous quench \cite{bayocboc2023frequency, bouchoule2016finite, thomas2021thermalization, bayocboc2022dynamics, simmons2020quantum .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In practical implementations, however, this ``sudden quench'' occurs over a finite duration \cite{schemmer2018monitoring .}

elements.sent-2411-244: We theoretically studied an interaction-driven quantum thermochemical engine (QTE) operating in an Otto cycle utilizing a harmonically trapped, weakly interacting 1D Bose gas as the working fluid.
elements.sent-2411-245: During the interaction-driven work strokes, the working fluid is treated as an isolated quantum many-body system undergoing dynamic evolution beginning from an initial thermal equilibrium state.
elements.sent-2411-246: To implement the thermalization strokes, the working fluid was modelled as an open quantum many-body system in diffusive contact with a thermal reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-247: This diffusive contact allowed us to perform additional chemical work (in addition to heat) on the working fluid through particle exchange with the reservoir, enabling operation as a QTE.
elements.sent-2411-248: We evaluated the finite-time performance of the QTE and quantified key figures of merit, focusing on its operation in the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime.
elements.sent-2411-249: In this regime, work strokes are implemented in the shortest possible time, driving the working fluid out of equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-250: This presents an opportunity to enhance the engine's power output due to fast driving.
elements.sent-2411-251: The performance of the out-of-equilibrium QTE was benchmarked against two scenarios: (i) an adiabatic (maximum-efficiency) QTE operating at zero temperature, as studied in reference ~\cite{keller2020feshbach ; and (ii) a quasistatic engine operating at realistic experimental temperatures, where work strokes are completed extremely slowly to achieve maximum finite-time efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-252: Our findings show that the zero-temperature QTE (studied in reference \cite{keller2020feshbach ) establishes an upper bound on the work and efficiency achievable by the proposed QTE operating with a finite-temperature Bose gas explored in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-253: Additionally, we explored the trade-off between efficiency and power output in the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime of operation.
elements.sent-2411-254: Remarkably, we found that the proposed QTE can operate at near-maximum efficiencies even in the out-of-equilibrium (sudden quench) regime.
elements.sent-2411-255: This high efficiency is achieved through the chemical work performed on the working fluid via particle inflow from the hot reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-256: Notably, near-maximum efficiencies were attained using a simple quench protocol without employing any optimization techniques such as the shortcut to adiabaticity used in reference ~\cite{keller2020feshbach .}
elements.sent-2411-257: When comparing the performance of the out-of-equilibrium engine to that of the quasistatic engine, which delivers the highest finite-time efficiency, we found that the drop in efficiency is relatively minor as the system transitions from quasistatic to sudden quench operation.
elements.sent-2411-258: This result indicates that the irreversible work produced in the sudden quench regime is not significant enough to severely impact finite-time performance.
elements.sent-2411-259: The out-of-equilibrium engine provides a favourable trade-off between power and efficiency, significantly outperforming the quasistatic engine in terms of efficient power, as it minimizes the efficiency loss per unit gain in power output.
elements.sent-2411-260: This study lays the groundwork for exploring the performance of the proposed QTE across a broader parameter space of 1D Bose gases.
elements.sent-2411-261: While our analysis focuses on the weakly interacting (quasicondensate) regime, 1D Bose gases exhibit a rich parameter space, as first identified in Refs.~\cite{kheruntsyan2003pair, kheruntsyan2005finite .}
elements.sent-2411-262: A natural extension of this work would be to investigate the performance of the out-of-equilibrium QTE in the strongly interacting Tonks-Girardeau regime.
elements.sent-2411-263: Although the $c$-field method used here is suitable for weakly interacting gases, employing theory of generalized hydrodynamics \cite{malvania2021generalized,bouchoule2022generalized could enable a comprehensive evaluation and comparison of the QTE’s performance across a wider range of interaction strengths in 1D Bose gases.}

elements.sent-2411-265: We examine the finite-time performance of the proposed quantum thermochemical engine in two extreme regimes: (i) the out-of-equilibrium regime (sudden quench), which yields near-maximum power (due to fast driving of the system) while compromising efficiency, and (ii) the quasistatic (near-adiabatic) limit, which approaches maximum efficiency but generates zero power output due to slow driving of the system.
elements.sent-2411-266: Notably, we show that using chemical work allows the engine to achieve efficiencies close to the near-maximum (quasistatic) limit, even in the out-of-equilibrium regime, while maintaining high power output.
elements.sent-2411-267: We also draw connections to previous research, particularly the case of an adiabatic engine cycle operating at zero temperature.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Operation as a QTE enabled via particle flow during thermalisation stroke with the hot reservoir
elements.sent-2411-299: The thermochemical Otto engine studied here is fundamentally different from a conventional Otto heat engine \cite{thermochemicalengine .}
elements.sent-2411-300: In a conventional heat engine, the system-reservoir coupling follows a canonical ensemble description, allowing only heat exchange between the working fluid and the reservoir.
elements.sent-2411-301: In contrast, a thermochemical engine operates under a grand-canonical ensemble \cite{marzolino2024quantum,luo2024critical,estrada2024quantum , enabling the exchange of both heat and particles (see, for example, \cite{nautiyal2024finite,eglinton2023thermodynamic, marzolino2024quantum, luo2024critical,estrada2024quantum ).}}
elements.sent-2411-302: In our proposed QTE, operation as a thermochemical engine is enabled via doing additional chemical work on the working fluid during the thermalization stroke AB.
elements.sent-2411-303: Specifically, chemical work is introduced through the inflow of $\Delta N$ particles from the hot reservoir into the working fluid as described in Sec.~\ref{stroke2 .}
elements.sent-2411-304: The inflow of $\Delta N$ particles increases the total particle number of the working fluid, which, in turn, results in the corresponding rise in the internal energy of the working fluid.
elements.sent-2411-305: This increase in energy of the working fluid can then be converted into mechanical work during the subsequent expansion stroke BC.
elements.sent-2411-306: Once this expansion work stroke is completed, the working fluid is brought into contact with the cold reservoir, allowing the same excess number of particles, $\Delta N$, to be transferred to the cold reservoir during the thermalization stroke CD (as described in Sec.~\ref{subsec:stroke4 ), thereby restoring the working fluid to its original state with the same initial number of particles.}
elements.sent-2411-307: The efficiency of this thermochemical Otto engine, defined as $\eta = -W/E_\mathrm{in}$, can be determined by evaluating the energy differences at the end of each stroke, as outlined in Sec.~\ref{subsec:numericmethod .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Unlike a conventional heat engine, where the energy, $E_\mathrm{in}$, supplied to the working fluid from the hot reservoir consists solely of heat transfer (through a canonical ensemble description), the energy supplied to the working fluid in the Otto thermochemical engine, $E_\mathrm{in = \langle \hat{H \rangle_{\mathbf{B -\langle \hat{H \rangle_{\mathbf{A >0$, includes a contribution from the chemical work (in addition to heat) and can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:chemplusheat} E_\mathrm{in} = Q_h + W_\mathrm{chem}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-309: Here, $Q_h$ represents the heat absorbed by the working fluid during the thermalization stroke AB with the hot reservoir, while $W_\mathrm{chem}$ accounts for the additional chemical work arising from the transfer of $\Delta N$ particles.
elements.sent-2411-310: If we adjust the chemical potential of the thermal reservoirs such that no net exchange of particles occurs, i.e., $\Delta N \simeq 0$, then $W_\mathrm{chem = 0$, and the net increase in the internal energy of the working fluid at the end of the thermalization stroke will be solely due to heat transfer, i.e., $E_\mathrm{in = Q_h$.}}
elements.sent-2411-311: In this work, we refer to this operational scheme, where $\Delta N \simeq 0$, as a quantum heat engine (QHE).
elements.sent-2411-312: Conversely, when $\Delta N \neq 0$, the engine operates as a quantum thermochemical engine (QTE) due to the additional chemical work facilitated through particle exchange.
elements.sent-2411-313: Determining the individual contributions of $Q_h$ and $W_{\mathrm{chem }$ to the total energy, $E_{\mathrm{in }$, is a nontrivial task due to the intrinsic coupling between heat and particle transport in ultracold atomic gases (see, e.g., \cite{brantut2013thermoelectric, husmann2018breakdown ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-314: However, we emphasize that the additional chemical work is accounted for in the total energetic cost when evaluating the efficiency of our proposed QTE by defining efficiency as $\eta = -W/E_\mathrm{in}$ rather than $\eta = -W/Q_h$.
elements.sent-2411-315: Accordingly, we simulate the unitary work strokes for the sudden quench regime within a finite time by linearly varying the interaction strength as in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:g_com ) and (\ref{eq:g_exp ), meeting the following criterion: \begin{equation}\label{suddentime} t_{\perp} \ll t_\mathrm{w} \ll t_{\parallel}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-316: The sudden quench regime, in which work strokes are implemented according to Eq.~(\ref{suddentime ), is referred to as the out-of-equilibrium regime in this work.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Since power, $P=-W/2t_\mathrm{w}$, the sudden quench engine provides an opportunity to operate at near-maximum power due to short driving time, $t_\mathrm{w}$ \cite{nautiyal2024classical,nautiyal2024finite,watson2024interaction .}
elements.sent-2411-318: This is followed by a detailed discussion of the key findings in subsequent sections, from section \ref{chemicalworkenabled to section \ref{ratioeffect .}}
elements.sent-2411-319: In this work, we extend the results of reference \cite{keller2020feshbach to finite (non-zero) temperatures using the $c$-field method, as outlined in Sec.~\ref{subsec:numericmethod .}}
elements.sent-2411-320: The efficient power, $\Theta$, is a relatively new performance criterion used to assess the trade-off between power and efficiency in real-world heat engines.

elements.sent-2411-364: In these appendices, we describe the differences between an ideal heat engine, such as the Carnot engine, and our proposed quantum thermal engine (QTE).
elements.sent-2411-365: Specifically, in Appendix~A, we show how, due to fundamental differences in the operational scheme, the Carnot efficiency is not a strict upper bound for a QTE, unlike for a traditional heat engine.
elements.sent-2411-366: Next, in Appendix~B, we describe the physical processes involved during the thermalization stroke of the QTE.
elements.sent-2411-367: Specifically, we detail the grand-canonical coupling between the working fluid and the thermal reservoir that facilitates the exchange of particles.

elements.sent-2411-371: In general, for any thermodynamic cycle, four thermodynamically allowed modes of operation exist \cite{watson2024interaction, buffoni2019quantum, bhattacharjee2021quantum : operation as an engine, refrigerator, accelerator, or heater (heat pump).}

elements.sent-2411-373: For an engine, the CoP is generally referred to as the efficiency, $\eta$.
elements.sent-2411-374: The ``benefit'' of operation is the net work output of the working fluid, while the ``cost'' of operation is the energy supplied to the working fluid from the hot reservoir.
